title: Unit 31 Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass: Ancient Remnants & Formal Registers

script.text: Welcome... to the Learn Greek Grammar Masterclass for Unit 31: Ancient Remnants. This is our final deep dive before the finish line. Even though we speak Modern Greek, the shadows of the ancient language are everywhere—in our law, our church, and our formal greetings. I'm William, and I'm joined by Athina. Athina... is it true that some Greeks still speak like Plato?
Not exactly like Plato, but we use

remnants. This was a 19th-century version of Greek that tried to bridge the gap between ancient and modern. It was the official language until 1976! So today, we have many 'fossils' in our daily speech.
Let's look at some phrases that every Greek student knows. Like
It means 'Okay' or 'In order'. It uses the ancient Dative case, which officially doesn't exist in Modern Greek! The word
We use it every single day.
is the Dative case too. In Modern Greek, we would say
But for religious or traditional phrases, we keep the ancient form. It sounds more respectful.
It adds a layer of solemnity. We also see this in formal verbs. Verbs that start with

Exactly. Many 'high-level' verbs used in news and books are very ancient. By learning these remnants, you aren't just learning a language... you are connecting with three thousand years of human thought.
Pro Tip: Think of these phrases like 'Latinisms' in English. Like saying 'Et cetera'. Athina, are there any ancient remnants that are actually... dramatic?
fire and fury. We use it when someone is extremely angry. It sounds like a scene from an ancient tragedy!
Drama and history. This concludes Unit 31. You are officially an elite student of the Greek continuum. Excellent work.
Σας συγχαίρω! Τα καταφέρατε! Γεια σας!
